issues.title: Add outline of Security Considerations Topics/sub-sections
issues.author: rahelFain
issues.body:
Provide an outline of the topics that will be discussed in this document. 
This could also include a statement noting that use case specific security considerations will need to be specified in their protocol specification document(s).

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
I began thinking of a few topics that could be included in the Security Considerations Section - but have listed them in this issue rather than a PR b/c I still have yet to discuss these with my co-authors. 

Potential Entries subsections to include:

1. Digital Emblems do not actively stop/defend against attacks: For emblems that indicate an asset's protected status, digital emblems can at best serve as a deterrent for attack - as there is no guarantee a would-be attacker will respect the laws, agreements or standards granting the asset its status. 

2. Plausible deniability surrounding whether or not emblem was seen, visible or verifiable at time of validation.

Note: This is by no means intended to be an exhaustive list of topics but rather a first brainstorm.

comments.author: amankin
comments.body: 3. For Digital Emblems that require Authentication of the Digital Emblem, origin authentication or other authentication of the Bearer, or Authorization of the Digital Emblem (the MUSTs in Section 3),  the threat model addressed is presentation of falsified Digital Emblems. Both 1 and 2 above will require addressing this threat model.

comments.author: ohmantics
comments.body: Several of the use cases would probably want to plan for forensics capabilities, such as a history of creation and modification of an emblem, associating it with internal references for identity of the creator/modifier, etc. This is not outward security, but merits some thinking about how external or encrypted-in-place metadata can be associated with an emblem.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body: Partially addressed in #7

issues.title: Bearer and asset question
issues.author: amankin
issues.body: This is an issue raised to me by Bill Woodcock, as he's tied up right now, but the paraphrase is mine:  could we clarify if there is a difference in meaning between a bearer of a digital emblem versus an asset that bears a digital emblem?  Since we are thinking of our definitions very much as working definitions, can we think of these as the same and in some cases use "asset?"

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body: I have thought about this too. In my head, the difference between bearer and asset is similar as in syntax/semantics. The bearer is, e.g., a domain name. The asset that is marked is identified by the bearer, and, e.g., could be the webserver identified by that domain name. But I agree, the two terms could benefit from clarification.

comments.author: dkg
comments.body: I think all bearers are assets, but not all assets are bearers.  For example, an asset that doesn't have an emblem isn't a bearer.

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body:
substantial discussion on the difference between an asset and a bearer. Some examples were given of likely differences.

action item to copy examples into comments in this issue.

ex: passport vs person it identifies, @dkg comments about things related to an asset, wetland might be an asset but not a bearer.

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body:
Stuart Cuard quoted from Black's law dictionary "A bearer is a **person** who...."  (indicating that a bearer is considered distinct from the things getting the special treatment.)

distinction between the thing entitled to special treatment and what presents it?

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body: Samit and Allison to propose text

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body: Believed to be incorporated in -01

issues.title: More details on use cases and requirements
issues.author: felixlinker
issues.body:
This issue is practically me crossposting my comments on the mailing list: https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/diem/nAX4K9D2HM5HNr92ozcQCs_J70o/

The use cases section is not detailed enough for actionable work in my opinion. We need to:

- Understand how each use case relates to the requirements on the architecture
- Decide whether use cases have received sufficient stakeholder evaluation and invite for that

The current version of the document is not specific enough to address both these goals.

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body: Example of the level of detail that @felixlinker proposes is in PR #5

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body: @felixlinker since PR #5 was merged is this issue still relevant? If so, what is remaining to do?

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body: @rohanmahy Yes, this issue is still relevant. Other use cases still miss the level of detail that is provided for the IHL use case. Also, we're still missing an understanding of which use case received stakeholder evaluation. But I did not add this for the IHL use case either. I guess this is best discusse on list.

issues.title: Do we want to respect the 5 author suggested limit?
issues.author: rohanmahy

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
No. Whomever is holding the pen needs to publish new documents, do needs to be in the author list.
Figure out if/what the authors need to be at the end.   The IETF actively discourages authorship to indicate endorsement.
But, other entities have different rules, and if we need someone listed as an author, then we should facilitate this.

comments.author: ohmantics
comments.body: #19 resolves this by adding a contributors section and moving Jim to it with his permission, leaving us at 5 authors.

issues.title: Is there a need for forensic records of emblems for some emblem types?
issues.author: rohanmahy
issues.body:
Suggested by @amankin 

@felixlinker said they intended to address this in Section 4.5

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body: @amankin Regarding this issue, we intend to discuss this as "Proof of Presence" in the "Extensions" section. Is there anything you're missing from that section?

comments.author: ts5746
comments.body:
As a suggestion for accountability or forensic use (probably need to find the proper terminology here), one approach could be to define different levels of proof to support different use cases and implementations, e.g.:

1. Could an actor (that is held accountable) have obtained the emblem, i.e. can it be proven that the emblem was available/discoverable and verifiable for an actor at some relevant moment in time?

2. Did the actor obtain the emblem, i.e. can it be proven that an actor actually discovered/accessed a verifiable emblem at a certain relevant moment in time?

3. Did the actor correctly validate the emblem, i.e. can it be proven that an actor actually validated the emblem correctly?
 
For example, under IHL, armed forces have an obligation to make an effort to adhere to the principles of proportionality and distinction, and to the prohibition on causing unnecessary suffering. Therefore, the weaker proof (level 1) might be sufficient in certain cases, if it can be proven the emblem existed and was (easily) accessible/obtainable for anyone, regardless of whether the actor actually did obtain the emblem.

Also, level 2/3 might not be compatible with use cases where discovery and validation must be undetected, but very reasonable or even required for other cases.

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
@ts5746 Thank you for the suggestion! 
I've created [a PR with some initial changes](https://github.com/ietf-wg-diem/diem-requirements/pull/25) based on it.

issues.title: Attacker and authorizing party roles and operations in use cases
issues.author: dkg
issues.body:
When describing a use case for a digital protocol, i'm used to seeing use cases presented from every party involved in a transaction.

For example, in digital id discussions, we need to talk about how a verifier operates, as well as how an issuer operates, and how a holder operates.

The use cases i'm seeing in the current document are all split out by talking about different bearer scenarios.

But i don't see any discussion of an attacker's perspective, or an authorizing party's perspective.  Should we detail those perspectives within each use case?  or should we detail them as their own distinct use case?

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
Thanks for suggesting that, dkg! What you raise here mirrors some issues I noticed when talking about the architecture to Jim on the hallway.

I think we should add those details to each use cases, but we should consider group very similar use cases together, and generalizing where possible. Depending on the feedback on individual use cases (e.g., press use case mostly received negative feedback), we might also save ourselves some work for use cases that we anticipate will not be in a WGLC draft.

issues.title: Diplomatic bag rules
issues.author: ohmantics
issues.body:
This use case was in early discussions but did not make it into this draft.

They're covered in article 27 of the 1961 [Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations](http://www.corpsdiplomatique.cd/VIENNA_CONVENTION_1961_ON_DIPLOMATIC_RELATIONS.pdf).

comments.author: mcr
comments.body: What is the digital footprint of a diplomatic bag.

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body:
> What is the digital footprint of a diplomatic bag.

The eventual footprint of DIEM is not restricted to digital-only assets, just the scope of our first mechanism.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
> > What is the digital footprint of a diplomatic bag.
> 
> The eventual footprint of DIEM is not restricted to digital-only assets, just the scope of our first mechanism.

That is very un-helpful.  
You are saying, the scope is larger than written, but then provide no guidance as to what the scope might be.
I can't write code or write security Playbooks around such a statement.
I can't evaluate if DNS can be used by a diplomatic bag.

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body:
> > > What is the digital footprint of a diplomatic bag.
> > 
> > 
> > The eventual footprint of DIEM is not restricted to digital-only assets, just the scope of our first mechanism.
> 
> That is very un-helpful. You are saying, the scope is larger than written, but then provide no guidance as to what the scope might be. I can't write code or write security Playbooks around such a statement. I can't evaluate if DNS can be used by a diplomatic bag.

Feel free to complain to the IESG that they did not charter the full original scope if you would like.

Ultimately we will have some type of data format the contains things like the type of emblem, what it applies to, etc. 
If the identity of the diplomatic bag is in the DNS, then there is no reason you couldn't use DNS to get a digital emblem for it.

comments.author: amankin
comments.body:
Below is some potential text, which doesn't have requirements language yet.  It was written a while back as part of research at my org.  We are discussing DIEM now with a couple of potential contributors (or at least quiet stakeholders) from foreign offices. 

Digital emblems can also protect diplomatic pouch shipments, diplomatic couriers, and diplomatic envoys, who are protected under the 1961 Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations, which states that they may not be stopped, delayed, or inspected. This creates the paradox that the validity of their credentials must be evaluated, yet doing so has historically compromised the very rights that are intended to be signaled. Diplomatic markings have also been misappropriated as cover for the smuggling of drugs and other contraband. Digital emblems, which can be validated instantaneously, at a distance, and without interrupting the subject, solve both of these problems, while streamlining and automating customs and immigrations processes.

comments.author: amankin
comments.body:
> (or at least quiet stakeholders)

By this I mean one or more individuals who consent to be acknowledged.

comments.author: amankin
comments.body: @mcr, right now, an expert inspection of external physical wrappings is used to validate that it's a diplomatic wrapping (to greatly over-simplify).  A digital component in the wrapping (preceded by a non-technical process that manages how it is or isn't enclosed in the wrapping) could provide a link out to DE and be able to be validated. This isn't much different from processes still to be defined under IHL use cases that permit/deny authorization to publish a DE.

issues.title: ISO shipping containers and related systems
issues.author: ohmantics
issues.body:
Early pre-WG use cases discussions included ISO shipping containers. They're covered by ISO 6346 with owner codes registered with [Bureau International des Containers](https://bic-code.org). The International Convention for Safe Containers established the [CSC Combined Data Plate](https://www.bic-code.org/csc-combined-data-plate/) which labels them for safety. Those come from the UN and International Maritime Organization.

https://www.bic-code.org/marking-of-containers/ goes into more detail about the ISO codes, CSC Combined Data Plate and includes an existing geofencing database used by IoT-enabled containers including a facility coding system established in ISO 9897, which itself is a coding system to describe the condition of a container including damage.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
1. Are these containers in motion, or ones sitting in some compound?
2. RFC7744.  The container is not the important part.  It's the communication to the tracking system, and the tracking system.
3. If the goal is to prevent digital attack on containers en-route, then the emblem will wind up applying to the ship.

I would make it out of scope.

issues.title: How should this draft expand on the Press case?
issues.author: amankin
issues.body:
The draft currently has a placeholder:

   Journalists in conflict zones use protective markings that indicate
   their status as a non-combatant.  Digital assets belonging to the
   press could be digitally marked, and protective markings in conflict
   zones could be digitized.

This does not even say clearly something that was articulated at IETF 124 by a stakeholder there
are distinct use cases here:  1. a digital emblem form of the PRESS marking worn by a person to mark
being a non-combatant; 2. a digital emblem marking on equipment (whether it about networked
computers/databases etc or about physical cameras, computers, sound equipment etc).  Let's use comments 
on this issue to flesh out 1. and 2. and also to reflect by the stakeholders about how much to develop these 
two sub-use-cases.

issues.title: Should we add CITES as a digital emblem use case?
issues.author: rohanmahy
issues.body: CITES: the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora  includes a system for tracking the legal movement of endangered species. If we can get stakeholder involvement I think this would be another good use case.

issues.title: Should whether an emblem supports or mandates forensic tracing be an extension or core consideration?
issues.author: rahelFain
issues.body:
2. I see the forensic tracking as a core requirement. That is to say, I don't think all use cases will need to support forensic tracing of emblem issuance/discoverability etc. but I think that every emblem's depiction should indicate whether or not it has an associated forensic tracing mechanism or ledger - and in cases where it does should include pointers to it.

_Originally posted by @rahelFain in https://github.com/ietf-wg-diem/diem-requirements/issues/25#issuecomment-3961344021_

2a. In addition to noting this as a core field, I think the question of whether or not a specific use case supports and/or requires the use of one or more forensic tracing mechanisms and the reasoning behind the decision(s) reached needs to be detailed in its security considerations. In cases where forensic tracing is optional I think the security considerations will also need to detail the risks vs. benefits of including it within the emblem

pulls.title: Add charter definitions
pulls.author: rahelFain
pulls.body: Explicitly adds the definitions for terms "(digital) emblem," bearer" and "validations" from the current charter.

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body: Response to feedback that the definitions in the charter could change in the event that the DIEM WG is rechartered sometime in the future.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
Thanks for preparing, @rahelFain, and iterating, @rohanmahy!

I must say, I don't like the "definition" of (digital) emblems in the charter. I don't find it to define anything. In the charter, this is not a problem, because we don't say: "Look, here we define what an emblem is." It's informal and glue text. This changes, however, if we copy-paste these passages into the document.

Let's look at what the definition says from a more abstract point of view:

> Emblems such as [...] can be [X]. Emblems can also be [Y].

The three sentences after that are more a problem statement that I would rather keep in the charter. These two sentences don't define anything, they only give examples of emblems.

Personally, I have two, contradictory goals:

- Have unambiguous terminology.
- Don't spend much time discussing what "digital emblems" are.

We already spent a long time during chartering trying to define digital emblems, and my feeling is that little came out of that. And I would rather sacrifice ambiguous terminology than having to spent a long time defining digital emblems.

What do you think? Is it really necessary to have a definition of digital emblems that encompass everything ever brought to the WG?

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
@felixlinker My intent in adding the definition text was not necessarily to state that this was the official definition, but rather to have the referenced text explicitly copied here so that it could be referenced even if the charter were to change. (Hence the comments noting that the text is copy-pasted from the current version of the charter.) 
I'd be willing to call this something less firm than a set of definitions (say for example descriptions) and to add a caveat that later documents may more precisely define these terms - and that the sole reason for including this text was to ensure document clarity.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body: If we move the text on DEs into the intro, that could work, for example. Just wanted to make clear that I think we should stay away from defining the term "DE." It' sa rabbit hole that we just got out off (chartering) :)

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body:
> If we move the text on DEs into the intro, that could work, for example. Just wanted to make clear that I think we should stay away from defining the term "DE." It' sa rabbit hole that we just got out off (chartering) :)

You could also use terms like "working definition" or "temporary definition" for now.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
> You could also use terms like "working definition" or "temporary definition" for now.

"Working definition" maybe, but "temporary" suggests we may want to revisit it. I think we shouldn't. This will just distract the working group without any benefit.

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body: Conflicts to be addressed, then OK to merge per IETF 124 discussion

```suggestion

# Requirements
```

whitespace matters in markdown!!

```suggestion
The definitions for terms "(digital) emblem," "bearer," and "validation" are reproduced from the charter {{CHARTER}} as of this writing.
```

"current" is redundant and eventually wrong..

```suggestion
```

This is no longer relevant since you have them in the next section.

```suggestion
```

These seem like requirements instead of part of the definition of "validation"

```suggestion
(Digital) Emblem:
: Emblems such as the Red Cross, Red Crescent, Red Crystal, and Blue Shield can be symbols of protection governed by International Humanitarian Law (IHL).
  Emblems can also be identified by other laws, agreements, or standards.
  There is a need to present emblems through digital communication channels.
  Emblems presented in such ways are called digital emblems.
  Digital emblems extend the range of identifying marks from the physical (visual and tactile) to the digital realm.
```

Turn into a markdown definition

```suggestion
Bearer:
: "To bear an emblem" means to present and be identified by a digital emblem.
  The entity that bears the emblem is the bearer or emblem holder.
  This is often a separate entity from the creator or original designer of the emblem.
```

Turn into a markdown definition

```suggestion

Validation:
: "To validate an emblem" means to confirm the authenticity or legitimacy of a particular symbol or design,
often by checking its details against a known standard or reference point.
  Validation may include ensuring that the bearer has not forged, stolen, or tampered with an emblem.
  Emblems may be observed by validators without the knowledge of the bearer presenting the emblem, or may be presented to a specific validator upon request.
  Cryptographic verification may or may not be used based on the in-place security mechanisms of the communication channel bearing the emblem.
  Which attributes an emblem contains, and how a digital emblem is secured and presented impacts how a validator interprets and trusts the assertions made within the emblem.
```

Turn into a markdown definition

reviews.author: felixlinker
reviews.body:
For easier reference, here my opinion as a review. I think, we're best off not aiming to define the term "digital emblem." Defining that term will just be a rabit chase without any benefit.

If we simply drop references to that term in this PR, I'm good with merging it!

pulls.title: Ready to Submit draft-ietf-diem-requirements-00 ??
pulls.author: rohanmahy
pulls.body: If the authors are ready to submit draft-ietf-diem-requirements-00 after the draft submission pause, just merge this PR and manually submit versioned/draft-ietf-diem-requirements-00.xml.

pulls.title: Simpler phrasing of undetectable validation
pulls.author: felixlinker
pulls.body: The old phrasing could have suggested that only *actual* bearers (and other parties) must not be able to detect validation. This is not what was intended. I now phrase undetectable validation w.r.t. to the discovery requirement. This should make it clear that also non-bearers must not be able to detect when someone is checking whether they are a bearer.

comments.author: dkg
comments.body:
I agree that we should change "bearers" to "potential bearers".

I don't see why we should drop issuers or authorizing parties.

```suggestion
Digital emblem discovery MAY require that potential bearers are unable to detect when an emblem is being discovered or validated.
```

pulls.title: Terminology and details on IHL use case
pulls.author: felixlinker

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
This pull request now incorporates a bunch of changes. I found it impossible to micro-manage parallel PRs, so this PR includes a bunch of changes to keep things simple. I think it's easier to iterate the document this way!

- It includes some terminology suggestions (edited by me, so re-review required) by Natasha Chabbra from the Australian Red Cross and Samit D'Cunha from the ICRC. Thus, addressing #6.
- It includes commits from now closed #15 to simplify some terminology.
- It partially addresses #14 and #11 for the IHL use case. There is now a stakeholder and domain model section, but critically, details on validators are missing. I will sync with Samit to get his input here.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body: Merged PR #4 into this one for clarity.

comments.author: felixlinker

this now says "under IHL" in two places. if the goal is to move it forward, perhaps we remove the second copy?
Thanks!

pulls.title: Simplify terminology
pulls.author: felixlinker
pulls.body:
Some simple changes, motivated by comments from @rohanmahy.

- Remove references to charter. I think this just complicates the document and serves no purpose.
- Remove references to requirements from charter.

Just makes everything tighter!

pulls.title: Asset/bearer
pulls.author: felixlinker
pulls.body:
I think we're best off defining asset/bearer in terms of who presents the emblem (bearer) and who the emblem applies to (asset).

For example, initially, bearers will always be domain names. However, the asset that is identified by them may not be domain names.

comments.body: Closes #6

comments.body: I think this should be merged with #8. Samit D'Cunha and Natasha Chabbra, for example, should also be contributors. Probably others too!

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body:
> I think this should be merged with #8. Samit D'Cunha and Natasha Chabbra, for example, should also be contributors. Probably others too!

The litmus test is if they contributed substantial text or not. Substantial text would be several paragraphs, a detailed outline, key ideas that were not independently contributed by someone else, a schema, a non-trivial example, etc.

pulls.title: New phrasing for assets under IHL
pulls.author: felixlinker
pulls.body: @ohmantics mentioned that he found the text on assets of emblems under IHL unclear. This PR is an attempt at clarifying that. Feedback appreciated!

pulls.title: Refactor use cases section
pulls.author: felixlinker
pulls.body:
This PR depends on #20.

Two thoughts were expressed by different participants:

1. We should list as many use cases as possible.
2. We should be clear which use cases has received sufficient stake holder input, in particular, when it comes to evaluating whether its requirements are accurate and there is interest in deployment.

This PR splits the use cases section in two: One for use cases with stakeholder evaluation, and one for use cases for future reference. This split allows us to track which use case received evaluation of its requirements, but also allows us to keep use cases for future reference, as well as be explicit about what we aim to target for the extensibility requirement.

comments.author: amankin
comments.body: It is not yet a meaningful change to create a section separating out use cases like this.  I support reviewing a table of use cases versus requirements that can include a column about whether there is currently a stakeholder who has approved acknowledgement and/or has been listed in contributors - and then the WG can determine if this split is useful enough.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
What do you think about this phrasing @rahelFain ? I tried integrating your idea "distinguishing between use cases where we have received stakeholder input and those where we haven't" - including more neutral tone.

@amankin I fear a table would lose too many details, so I prefer to suggestion from Rahel.

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
I wanted to be sure to follow up on this PR, since I have been looking it over and thinking about what changes I think still need to be made. 
For the time being, I figured I'd share my thoughts. 
Overall, these changes largely seem to center on the balance between the inclusion of use cases where we are unable to get stakeholder input, or where stakeholder input is only obtained after this document has reached WG last call. 
Looking over the minutes from our previous meetings, particularly those from [IETF 123](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/minutes-123-diem-202507221500/), I do see that the majority of those who voted in the show of hands indicated that those use cases can be excluded (29 for, 13 against, 2 no opinion, 25 present but did not vote). 

However, the subsequent discussion raised the point that 
 If a stakeholder comes at a later time (e.g. after the document has reached WG last call), we cannot say "too late." This is particularly important as some stakeholders are very difficult to find and/or get ahold of. (In some cases merely identifying who the right stakeholders are  can prove challenging.)

Ultimately, the chairs determined they would need to work on a way to get more stakeholders involved. 
However, I do not remember us ever reaching any sort of consensus on how to handle cases where one or more stakeholders give us input on their (respective) use case's requirements after this document has been completed/has reached WG last call. 

To this end, my best suggestion is that we first introduce all of the use cases and later on go into more detail about the extent of stakeholder input we have received as of this writing as well as more detailed descriptions of the use cases for which we have received more stakeholder input. 
I also think we need to devise a system through which input from stakeholders who join the conversation at a later date can still be noted and taken into account. 

Coming back to the revisions at hand: What I am really wary of is listing the use cases for which we have received input first or putting more emphasis on these from the get-go (either explicitly or implicitly) because to the best of my understanding, the WG must not/ is expressly not allowed to state or imply that any use case is better or more worthy of being considered than another. 
To that end, I like Allison's suggestion of including a matrix indicating the level of stakeholder input received as of this document's writing - perhaps as a way to introduce the more detailed accounts of the different use cases.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
You write "the WG must not/ is expressly not allowed to state or imply that any use case is better or more worthy of being considered than another." Where does that come from? My concern is that we *cannot* address certain use cases because they're underspecified. How are we supposed to account for the "United Nations Peacekeepers" use case if all we have in writing on it is: "UN Peacekeepers use protective markings in theater as well as facilities associated with the mission." How does that inform protocol development? How does this use case relate to our requirements and domain model?

Your concern seems to be that use cases may receive stakeholder evaluation after a WGLC. I think we can mitigate that risk easily because the WG is always free to change course. Should a stakeholder join the discussion one year into protocol development, we can simply write a new document that details that use case and have it adopted by the WG. So I don't see any risks here!

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
I want to make a distinction between saying a use case is more worthy of being considered for implementation and whether or not it's feasible for a use case to be implemented at a given time.  

As I understand it, the intent is for this document to be finalized upon reaching WG last call. This means that any statements about which use case is more worthy of being considered or implemented would outlast any changes in the WG's scope, and would therefore effectively be value statements saying _use case x is the more important or the most important_ regardless of what future information we may obtain surrounding the needs motivating other use cases and  the respective impacts of standardization of digital emblems for them. 

Think for example, if we had initially gotten extensive input and interest for the RAMSAR use case and less for that of ICRC one and therefore failed to mention the ICRC use case in the primary use cases and/or limited its mention to a single sentence in an appendix.  It seems pretty clear how these actions could convey an implicit message that the RAMSAR use case is in an absolute sense, a better use case, is more worthy of implementation than the ICRC's etc..
Given the extent of what we do know about the ICRC's pressing need for digital emblems, their interest in its standardization/development and the impact it stands to have, it seems pretty clear how conveying that sort of message would not only be unwise but also inflammatory and would constitute us overstepping our role as technologists. 

To be clear, I am not saying the WG cannot or should not decide to first define or implement digital emblems for the use cases for which we currently have more stakeholder input or involvement and a better defined set of requirements. (After all it will be more feasible to do this in these cases.) All I am saying is that that decision should be independent of the extent to which we mention the other previously identified use cases in this document.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
I totally get your point on distinguishing use cases "more" and "less" worthy, which is why I changed the language on that. Now, the section only says that one section contains use cases that have been evaluated and use cases that have not.

But I also understand the WGLC, document writing process, and WG scope differently. My understanding is that you can always change scope, write new documents, etc. After WGLC, *this* document is finished, but I think nothing would change the WG to write a new document that lists additional use cases. Think about how many RFCs have been amended/superseded. I don't think there will be any issues with use cases that receive their evaluation after WGLC. But I will confirm my understanding with the chairs!

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body: I don't see any benefit to merging this PR. It is unlikely we will get stakeholder input if we make it seem as if some artificial deadline has passed. I suggest closing the PR.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body: What deadline are you referring to? WGLC? I want "evaluated" be interpreted w.r.t. time of writing. I could clarify this. Does that mean you would also argue against, e.g., a table that documents stakeholder input?

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body:
@felixlinker 

> What deadline are you referring to? WGLC? I want "evaluated" be interpreted w.r.t. time of writing. I could clarify this. Does that mean you would also argue against, e.g., a table that documents stakeholder input?

By deadline I was referring to the draft submission deadline (today) or another similarly arbitrary date. If we are ready for WGLC that is another story.

I think a table of stakeholder would be useful, possibly in an Appendix. 

However, I think the categorization you propose would just result in make-work without any particular clarity or benefit for the WG. We know we need to get more stakeholder input on several of the use cases, and that is happening (albeit slowly).

reviews.author: rahelFain
reviews.body:
I think this restructuring is premature.

As I see it there are use cases within the initial architecture/initial scope and others we can work on later. Prioritizing them seems to go against the entire point of this document.
As noted in the minutes from 123

"Chairs - Expect use cases and requirements doc to be relatively
comprehensive and not restricted to the charter. Prioritisation will
take place as the protocol specification is worked on, and the protocol
specification will be constrained by the charter."
(from IETF 123 minutes: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/minutes-123-diem-202507221500/ )
In practice I do think it is worth distinguishing between use cases where we have received stakeholder input and those where we haven't but would like the tone of this to be more neutral - i.e. simply stating factually that we did/did not get stakeholder input and possibly the extent of input we received.

pulls.title: Update draft-ietf-diem-requirements.md to put Rahel and Felix names f…
pulls.author: amankin
pulls.body:
…irst in editors

This is in response to a discussion among those of us working on the JHUAPL/PCH prototype project

reviews.author: felixlinker
reviews.body: I don't mind this change, but does it change anything? I'm not aware of any semantics being attached to the order of authors.

pulls.title: expand upon proof of presence
pulls.author: rahelFain
pulls.body:
Add text to flesh out the definition(s) of proof of presence (currently §4.5 of the draft). 
Additional details and considerations are still needed. I will list these in subsequent comments and follow-up issues as necessary.

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body: For the time being I've left the placement and title of the section unchanged. However, I have a few additional changes and thoughts on this. I'll list them as separate comments - since I think many of them warrant their own discussion.

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
1. I think the section title needs to be updated:
Since this update outlines three different levels of forensic tracking or of demonstrating a violation of an emblem's associated requirements, statutes or provisions, proof of presence does not seem to encompass the extent of what may be proven or established by these.

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body: 2. I see the forensic tracking as a core requirement. That is to say, I don't think all use cases will need to support forensic tracing of emblem issuance/discoverability etc. but I think that every emblem's depiction should indicate whether or not it has an associated forensic tracing mechanism or ledger - and in cases where it does should include pointers to it.

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body: 2a. In addition to noting this as a core field, I think the question of whether or not a specific use case supports and/or requires the use of one or more forensic tracing mechanisms and the reasoning behind the decision(s) reached needs to be detailed in its security considerations. In cases where forensic tracing is optional I think the security considerations will also need to detail the risks vs. benefits of including it within the emblem.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
Hi Rahel!

Levels 2 and 3 seem to conflict with undetectable validation. How do you see that conflict to play out? Also, I see a slight tension between the heading (_proof_ of presence) and the body, which simply says "establish." That seems weaker. I don't think this is a problem really, because we probably want a weaker notion in general (requiring that something is provable is tough), but we may want to rephrase the header.

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body:
> Hi Rahel!
> 
> Levels 2 and 3 seem to conflict with undetectable validation. How do you see that conflict to play out? Also, I see a slight tension between the heading (_proof_ of presence) and the body, which simply says "establish." That seems weaker. I don't think this is a problem really, because we probably want a weaker notion in general (requiring that something is provable is tough), but we may want to rephrase the header.

Not all requirements will be the same for every use case. We need to have a set of tools that will not all be able to be used simultaneously.

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body: I am really not keen at all on the term "violator". If there is consensus to keep this section, could we please make this a bit more neutral, like "accessor" or "querying party"?

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
> Not all requirements will be the same for every use case. We need to have a set of tools that will not all be able to be used simultaneously.

Agreed, but the PR explicitly mentions emblems that mark assets as protected, hence, the risk for conflict with undetactable validation.

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body:
> > Not all requirements will be the same for every use case. We need to have a set of tools that will not all be able to be used simultaneously.
> 
> Agreed, but the PR explicitly mentions emblems that mark assets as protected, hence, the risk for conflict with undetactable validation.

I agree that some of the language in the PR is problematic. I suggested starting with "violator". Hopefully that will result in an improvement. Note also that "protected" does not always equal "protected under IHL".

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
Thanks for the feedback. I went ahead and added a caveat noting the potential conflict between Level 2 and Level 3 and replaced the term "alleged violator" with "querying party".

Regarding the conflict terminology - "proof of presence" vs "establish":
I intentionally used the terms "establish" because I was not sure the term proof was appropriate. 
If we agree to consider the term proof to be fully definitive as in the definition of a mathematical proof, I agree that the term is too strong. If we define proof more colloquially, then proving and establishing could be considered synonymous.

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
> Regarding the conflict terminology - "proof of presence" vs "establish": I intentionally used the terms "establish" because I was not sure the term proof was appropriate. If we agree to consider the term proof to be fully definitive as in the definition of a mathematical proof, I agree that the term is too strong. If we define proof more colloquially, then proving and establishing could be considered synonymous.

I should also clarify that as of this writing I have not come up with other more appropriate options for the section title. I will keep thinking about that and am also open to suggestions.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body: Thanks for the changes, Rahel! Are you aware of any use cases that require levels 2+3?

PLEASE pay attention to whitespace. You need a newline between different types of markup.

```suggestion
Level 3 -  presence, verifiability access and verification: Demonstrating presence verifiability and access and that the alleged violator verified the emblem upon accessing it.

```
